{{Short description|Reformulation of classical mechanics using the Lagrangian function}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Lagrangian mechanics
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Classical mechanics]], [[Newton's laws of motion]], [[Conservation of energy]], [[Noether's theorem]]
| key_equations = [[Euler–Lagrange equation]]
}}

'''Lagrangian mechanics''' is a reformulation of [[classical mechanics]] introduced by the Italian-French mathematician [[Joseph-Louis Lagrange]] in 1788. It describes the motion of a system in terms of its [[Lagrangian (physics)|Lagrangian function]] <math>L</math>, defined as the difference between [[kinetic energy]] <math>T</math> and [[potential energy]] <math>V</math>:
:<math>L = T - V</math>

Unlike [[Newtonian mechanics]], which works with vectors and forces, Lagrangian mechanics works with scalars and [[generalized coordinate]]s, making it particularly powerful for systems with constraints.

== Principle of least action ==
{{Main|Principle of least action}}
The fundamental principle of Lagrangian mechanics is that the system evolves along a path that minimizes (or more precisely, makes stationary) the [[action (physics)|action]] <math>S</math>:
:<math>S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt</math>
:<math>\delta S = 0</math>

== Euler–Lagrange equations ==
Applying the calculus of variations yields the [[Euler–Lagrange equation]]s for each generalized coordinate <math>q_i</math>:
:<math>\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0</math>

For a particle in a potential, this reduces to [[Newton's second law]]:
:<math>m\ddot{q} = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial q}</math>

== Connection to Noether's theorem ==
{{Main|Noether's theorem}}
The Lagrangian formulation directly relates continuous [[symmetry (physics)|symmetries]] of the Lagrangian to [[conservation law]]s via [[Noether's theorem]]:
* Time-translation invariance → conservation of [[energy]]
* Spatial-translation invariance → conservation of [[momentum]]
* Rotational invariance → conservation of [[angular momentum]]

== Generalized momenta ==
The [[conjugate momentum]] associated with coordinate <math>q_i</math> is:
:<math>p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}</math>

If the Lagrangian does not depend on a particular coordinate <math>q_i</math> (the coordinate is "cyclic"), then the corresponding momentum is conserved.

== Advantages ==
* Naturally handles constraints through the choice of generalized coordinates
* Independent of the choice of coordinate system
* Extends straightforwardly to [[special relativity]] and [[quantum mechanics]] (via the [[path integral formulation]])
* Forms the basis for [[Noether's theorem]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Classical mechanics]]
* [[Hamiltonian mechanics]]
* [[Newton's laws of motion]]
* [[Noether's theorem]]
* [[Principle of least action]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Lagrangian mechanics| ]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Calculus of variations]]
